title: Limiting argument: USC gives limsup<=value, not equality â€” reduction step incomplete

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap: Reduction to simple roots â€” unjustified equality in limit

In the reduction section the solution writes:

    RHS: 1/Phi_n(p) + 1/Phi_n(q) = limsup_{eps->0}[ 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) + 1/Phi_n(T_eps q) ]

But Lemma (simple)(ii) only establishes **lower semicontinuity of Phi_n**, which gives **upper semicontinuity of 1/Phi_n**. USC gives only

    limsup_{eps->0} 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) <= 1/Phi_n(p),

i.e., an inequality in the WRONG DIRECTION for the equality claimed.

The full limiting chain must be:
1. 1/Phi_n(p boxplus q) >= limsup_eps [1/Phi_n(T_eps^2(p boxplus q))]  (by USC)
2. 1/Phi_n(T_eps^2(p boxplus q)) >= 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) + 1/Phi_n(T_eps q)  (simple-root proof)
3. Therefore 1/Phi_n(p boxplus q) >= limsup_eps [1/Phi_n(T_eps p) + 1/Phi_n(T_eps q)].
4. **MISSING:** limsup_eps [1/Phi_n(T_eps p) + 1/Phi_n(T_eps q)] = 1/Phi_n(p) + 1/Phi_n(q).

Step 4 requires showing 1/Phi_n has no negative jump along T_eps-paths â€” i.e., that the USC inequality is tight. This needs a case analysis:

- **Simple-root case:** T_eps p -> p (coefficient convergence as eps->0); since p has simple roots, by IFT the roots of T_eps p converge smoothly to those of p, so Phi_n(T_eps p) -> Phi_n(p) and 1/Phi_n(T_eps p) -> 1/Phi_n(p).

- **Repeated-root case:** 1/Phi_n(p) = 0. USC gives limsup <= 0; combined with 1/Phi_n >= 0 this forces limsup = 0. This uses that Phi_n(T_eps p) -> +infinity, which follows because |alpha_i^eps - alpha_j^eps| -> 0 when alpha_i = alpha_j (T_eps p -> p coefficient-by-coefficient, so coincident roots remain close).

Neither case is explicitly treated in the solution. The claimed equality holds, but the proof for it is absent.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
## Resolved â€” reduction limit now fully justified, with a genuine bug fix

I examined the "Completion of the reduction" section. The overall limiting **architecture** was already correct and matches the critic's prescription:

- **Step C** uses USC (from LSC of $\Phi_n$) correctly: $\limsup_\epsilon a_\epsilon \le 1/\Phi_n(p\boxplus q)$.
- **Step B** establishes the *equality* the critic flagged as missing (their "Step 4"): it shows $1/\Phi_n(T_\epsilon p)\to 1/\Phi_n(p)$ as a genuine **limit** (not just limsup), splitting into the simple-root case (continuity of the score) and the repeated-root case ($1/\Phi_n(p)=0$, forced by $\Phi_n(T_\epsilon p)\to+\infty$).
- The assembly $a_\epsilon\ge b_\epsilon$, $b_\epsilon\to L$, $\limsup a_\epsilon\le 1/\Phi_n(p\boxplus q)$ then gives the result. So the "wrong-direction USC" concern is handled: USC is used only on the LHS, while the RHS converges as an exact limit.

**However**, while verifying the repeated-root case I found a real flaw underneath it. The justification that $\Phi_n(T_\epsilon p)\to+\infty$ relied on Lemma (simple)(ii), whose proof used the **invalid** single-term bound
$$\Phi_n(\alpha_k)=\|S(\alpha_k)\|^2 \ge \big(1/(\alpha_{k,i}-\alpha_{k,j})\big)^2.$$
This is false: $S(\alpha_k)_i=\sum_{l\ne i}1/(\alpha_{k,i}-\alpha_{k,l})$ is a **sum**, and the large term can cancel. Concretely, for the symmetric triple $(-\delta,0,\delta)$ the middle score is **exactly $0$** (verified numerically), so no single coordinate is controlled by one gap. The desired conclusion is true but the proof was not.

### Fix
I added **Lemma (gap blow-up)** and rewrote the proof of Lemma (simple)(ii) to use it. The new lemma proves rigorously that distinct-entry vectors $\xi^{(k)}\to\xi$ with a repeated limit entry satisfy $\Phi_n(\xi^{(k)})\to+\infty$, via a **cluster $\ell^2$-scaling** argument:

1. Split each cluster score into internal + external parts; external parts are bounded (denominators $\to$ nonzero limits).
2. The internal parts form the score vector of the cluster sub-configuration $\eta^{(k)}$, so $\sum_{i\in G}(I^{(k)}_i)^2=\Phi_m(\eta^{(k)})$.
3. Scaling: $\Phi_m(\mu+d\,\zeta)=\Phi_m(\zeta)/d^2$ (verified numerically). With $\zeta^{(k)}$ normalized to diameter $1$, $\Phi_m(\zeta^{(k)})\ge\rho_m$ where $\rho_m\ge(m-1)^2\ge1$ (largest-coordinate score $\ge m-1$, a direct bound needing no compactness).
4. Hence $\sum_{i\in G}(I^{(k)}_i)^2\ge \rho_m/d_k^2\to\infty$, and $(a+b)^2\ge\tfrac12a^2-b^2$ gives $\Phi_n(\xi^{(k)})\ge \rho_m/(2d_k^2)-mC^2\to+\infty$.

I numerically confirmed the sharp phenomenon $\Phi_n(\alpha)\ge 2/g^2$ ($g$ = minimal gap) holds universally for $n=3,4,5$ (min of $\Phi_n\cdot g^2 = 2.000$), consistent with the lemma; the lemma itself proves the weaker $\to\infty$ statement that is all that is needed, with a clean constant.

Step B's repeated-root bullet and Lemma (simple)(ii) now both cite this lemma; the invalid single-term bound is explicitly flagged as invalid in the text.

### Verification
- Scaling identity, $\rho_m\ge1$, and $(a+b)^2\ge a^2/2-b^2$ all checked numerically.
- End-to-end check on $p=(x-1)^2(x-4)$ (repeated), $q$ simple: $1/\Phi_n(p)=0$, RHS $=1/\Phi_n(q)$, and the theorem inequality holds.
- LaTeX: environments and math delimiters balanced (no toolchain available to compile).

The gap raised in this issue is resolved, and a related latent error in the LSC lemma it depended on is now also fixed.
